home.heroTitleLine1: A digital spot for playful learning materials
home.heroLine2b: free materials so anyone—from toddlers to adults—can learn at an easy, playful pace.
home.cardTapColoringTip:
A tap-to-fill coloring toy for early learners.
Enjoy instant cause-and-effect through color play.
home.cardPopPopBubblesTip:
Tap floating bubbles to burst sparkles and little animals.
A gentle, intuitive game designed for kids.
home.cardPairLinkTip:
Connect matching numbers without crossing lines.
Fill the board through trial and error.
home.cardReflecTip:
Predict reflections and route the shot to goal.
A compact puzzle for spatial intuition.
home.cardSkyTip:
Use edge clues to infer hidden building heights.
A clean puzzle for layered logical thinking.
home.cardPresTitle: Pres-Sure Judge (プレッシャージャッジ)
home.cardPresTip:
Balance the scale under time pressure.
Placement and timing decide each round.
home.sectionAboutBody:
These puzzles and apps started as homemade projects so we would not have to assign endless extra worksheets when books and classroom printouts were no longer enough. We brought them to the web so they run in your browser.

If something looks fun, tap in and try it freely!

※For this browser release we use AI-assisted generation in places. You may notice occasional awkward visuals or behavior—thanks for your understanding.
home.sectionUsageBeforeContact: All games are free. Rules and controls are on each screen. Contact us via the
home.sectionUsageMid: . For terms and privacy, see the

reflecShot.boardHelp:
Bounce off bumpers for gems! Collect the target count, then reach the goal.
Click or tap bumpers to rotate them, or change their angle by drawing a path through a cell.
Double-click or double-tap the projectile—or swipe—to launch!
reflecShot.infoGoal: Spatial reasoning, reflection rules, and stepwise strategy updates.
reflecShot.infoOperation: Tap to select cells; press and hold, then swipe to set bumper directions.
reflecShot.helpOpenAria: Show how to play and rules
reflecShot.helpPcNote: On desktop, clicks and drawing a path on cells work the same way.
reflecShot.ruleIntro: Bounce off bumpers for gems! Collect the target count, then reach the goal.
reflecShot.ruleControls:
Click or tap bumpers to rotate them, or change their angle by drawing a path through a cell.
Double-click or double-tap the projectile—or swipe—to launch!
reflecShot.ruleControlsPc: Rotate bumpers: click/tap or draw through a cell. Launch: double-click/tap or swipe.
reflecShot.resultFailMessage: Sorry—you didn't make it to the goal…
reflecShot.resultFailGems: Not enough gems. Bounce off bumpers to collect gems before entering the goal.
reflecShot.ruleGemsBasic: Bounce off bumpers for gems! Collect the target count, then reach the goal.
reflecShot.ruleCrossBonus: Revisit a cell twice, pass straight through both times, and enter from perpendicular directions—then you get 1 gem there.
reflecShot.ruleTwoSidedBonus: Hit both sides of a bumper for a bigger haul (+3 bonus gems, 4 total from that hit).
reflecShot.debugInitialWrongRateGrade12: Initial facing: G1 fixes 1 wrong / 1 correct among 2 path bumpers. G2 (≥4 bumpers) fixes 1–2 wrong, rest correct (not %-based).
reflecShot.debugHashHelpMid: encodes grade and seed only (same initial board as generation). Legacy
reflecShot.debugGrade2Mid: Grade2 bend-6 mid search (target {n} bends total; tail hook adjusted)

pairLink.ruleHint: Drag to connect matching numbers and fill every cell. Larger sizes may take a few seconds to generate.

presSure.intro: Drag weights from inventory onto the pans. Stay balanced within 10 seconds. Wrong judgments stack in this survival game.
presSure.resultSubtitle: From the ground (R1) upward — full history of {n} turns

skyscraper.rulesIntro: Tap/click a cell to cycle 1→2→…→N→blank. Keys 1–N and Backspace work. Swipe up to increase, down to decrease.
skyscraper.rulesLi1: Each cell is a building height; enter digits 1…N.
skyscraper.rulesLi2: Each row and column contains 1…N exactly once (no duplicates).
skyscraper.rulesLi3: Edge clues are how many buildings you see from that direction (count increases whenever a taller building appears in front).
skyscraper.rulesLi4: You win when all clues are satisfied.

st.checkOk: No rule conflicts found. Keep going.
st.rowClueMismatch: Row {n} does not match the visibility clues.
st.colClueMismatch: Column {n} does not match the visibility clues.

err.expiredNew: This puzzle expired. Please generate a new one.
err.mismatchNew: Puzzle mismatch. Please generate a new one.

hiddenStack.debugMeshCrevice: Visual mesh scale (crease fill; physics unchanged)
hiddenStack.hintSwipe: Drag the board to rotate slightly. Touch the row to pick a number immediately, then slide sideways without lifting.
hiddenStack.howToBody: Drag the board to rotate a little left or right. Touch the bottom block row to select instantly, then keep sliding sideways to adjust your answer in real time.

pairLink.p_dev: Pair-Link (Numberlink) links matching endpoints with non-crossing paths that visit every cell—three coupled constraints in a classic paper puzzle. On paper, erasing is slow and feedback lags. In Wispo, drag-draw and undo are instant so retries do not break the rhythm of thought. Stylus or finger input ties fine motor control to spatial imagery, continuous with school-age drawing tasks.
pairLink.p_neuro: Searching under both no-crossing and full-grid rules repeatedly engages error prediction and strategy updating in the prefrontal cortex. Holding a tentative path in working memory while lookahead checks dead-ends loads visuospatial working memory—akin to map reading and net reasoning. Well-paced repetition supports schema formation, not rote memorization.
pairLink.p_mont: Montessori demonstrations show only minimal order (“no crossings,” “no gaps”); path discovery stays with the child. Rule breaks return immediately as interaction, enabling self-correction without adult scolding—consistent with auto-education in a prepared environment.
pairLink.p_step: Grades scale board size, pair count, and generator constraints. Early boards keep the search space small for achievable wins; later grades demand longer hypothesis maintenance and planning. Viewing lines as graph edges seeds discrete-math intuition. When adults scaffold, naming “where it gets risky next” supports metacognition more than giving answers.

skyscraper.p_dev: Skyscraper combines Latin-square uniqueness with edge “visible skyscraper” counts. Paper erasure is tedious; digital play makes trial verification cheap so attention stays on constraint reasoning. One rule set supports children through adults by varying depth, not mechanics.
skyscraper.p_neuro: Each contradiction triggers prediction-error processing and frontal update loops. Simulating occlusion from a bird’s-eye view couples spatial inference with magnitude comparison. “Maybe” placements externalize working memory, scaffolding the distinction between hypotheses and committed digits—useful persistence for case analysis in math and science.
skyscraper.p_mont: Order, precision, and concentration map to a calm board that reflects each action—external order supporting inner order. Maybe-mode is support, not substitution: the child remains the decision-maker, matching self-education principles.
skyscraper.p_step: Board size and presets widen the search space gradually. Small boards make strong clue patterns tangible; larger boards build tolerance for compound constraints—parallel to sustained case-splitting stamina in arithmetic.

reflecLab.p_dev: Reflec-Shot abstracts mirrors and walls into a grid with bumper symbols (／＼－｜), letting learners decide trajectories logically. Physical kits cost setup and safety; angles vary. Digital motion applies reflection rules exactly, turning hypotheses into visible outcomes in tight feedback loops.
reflecLab.p_neuro: Clear success/failure per trial rehearses outcome monitoring. Predicting cell-wise motion updates direction sequences, loading working memory like maze tasks. Long-press then swipe to commit a bumper type links motor planning with rapid strategy choice—near cognitive flexibility training. Explicit goal rules ease error attribution.
reflecLab.p_mont: Consistent environmental feedback matches materials that make errors obvious without lecturing. Children map failures to their own action sequences, reducing adult micromanagement. Demonstrations can show only the minimal order of reflection; exploration remains the child’s work.
reflecLab.p_step: Higher grades add cells and bumper complexity, moving from single-bounce prediction to multi-bounce chains. Early clears support normalized ego strength (the feeling of completing work independently); harder stages train sustained attention and planning horizons. The lab build invites small-scale observation before wider classroom use.

presSure.p_dev: Pres-Sure Judge centers on serial decisions under time pressure: the NPC places weights first, then the player drags from inventory to the left or right pan and must stay balanced within the limit. Paper or still images rarely convey a tipping scale and a countdown at once, and they make it hard to accumulate consequences of misjudgment across turns. In the browser, near-physical feedback and the timer stay in sync so magnitude, placement, and timing can be rehearsed as one continuous action. Survival-style accumulation of wrong judgments or timeouts across rounds is meant to stress sustained strategy and self-regulation more than single-shot correctness.
presSure.p_neuro: Choosing whether to place a weight, which pan to use, and when to press Judge within a short window repeatedly engages executive functions linked to the prefrontal cortex—especially inhibitory control (holding back impulsive taps or premature commits) and rapid updating of the situation. Coupling finger trajectories with a moving scale recruits visuomotor integration; holding each weight’s value alongside left–right torque imagery doubles working-memory load. Because the NPC reshapes the state every move, prediction errors are processed often, strengthening trial-and-error loops through frontal–subcortical circuits. Longer runs also tax arousal and sustained attention, placing the task close to endurance training for school-age executive skills.
presSure.p_mont: Montessori emphasizes causality grounded in concrete materials; here, tilt of the scale returns as embodied cause-and-effect before abstract lecture. The screen acts as a prepared environment: misplacements and timeouts appear immediately, offering environmental feedback that supports self-correction without scolding. The NPC stands in for another agent’s move—a thin but clear social frame for “how do I respond after someone else acts?” When adults scaffold, naming which pan looks heavier or what the child fears on the next move supports self-observation more than narrating outcomes for them.
presSure.p_step: As rounds advance, NPC pressure and inventory tension shift play from single-weight comparisons toward imagining balance several moves ahead. The Judge action demands a meta-decision to stop tweaking and commit, helping separate impulse from reasoning. Difficulty rises in steps to preserve a prepared gradient of load so learning can be drawn from both success and failure. Alongside logic-puzzle titles, the design offers integrated practice with number, time, and bodily action.

tapColoring.summary: Educational benefits of this learning material
tapColoring.p_dev: Tap coloring integrates two threads for toddlers: grasping cause-and-effect and emotionally responding to color in a digital medium. Where analog coloring stresses motor inhibition (“stay inside the lines”), this prototype maximizes immediate visual reward—touched areas fill right away. That tight feedback loop can serve as an early interface for digital-native children to build a sense of self-efficacy.
tapColoring.p_neuro: Looking at the screen while tapping a region recruits visuomotor coordination between vision and hand. The thick, “juicy” ink feedback gives a sense of space being filled, which can engage visual cortex and dopamine-related reward circuits. Choosing one color among several also offers a light exercise in simple decision-making supported by the prefrontal cortex.
tapColoring.p_mont: The design leans on correction from the environment (auto-education). Bold black outlines act as clear boundaries so children can see at once where their action took effect. Rather than adults dictating “color here,” the flow respects spontaneous intent—“I’ll make this spot red next”—followed by self-evaluation of the outcome.
tapColoring.p_step: At the lowest grades, play centers on simple silhouettes and single-color fills to stack small successes. As difficulty rises, motifs become more segmented (e.g., from an animal’s face toward the whole body), and layouts invite separating adjacent regions by color, bridging toward ideas of color composition. This mirrors Montessori sensory education: concrete manipulation first, then gradual movement toward more abstract aesthetic judgment.

privacy.title: Privacy Policy
privacy.intro: Wispo (“we,” “our,” or “this site”) describes how we handle personal information for services offered on this website (“the Service”) in this Privacy Policy (“this Policy”).
privacy.s1Body: “Personal information” means personal information as defined under applicable privacy law: information relating to a living individual that can identify a specific person, such as name, date of birth, address, telephone number, contact details, or other descriptions.
privacy.s2Body: We may ask for personal information such as your name or email address when you use contact forms or similar features. We may also collect access logs using technologies such as cookies as you use the Service.
privacy.s3Intro: We collect and use personal information for the following purposes:
privacy.purpose1: To provide and operate the Service
privacy.purpose2: To respond to inquiries from users (including identity verification where needed)
privacy.purpose3: To contact you about maintenance, important notices, and similar matters when necessary
privacy.purpose4: To identify users who violate our terms or attempt to misuse the Service, and to refuse such use
privacy.adsP1: This site uses Google AdSense, a third-party ad network. We and third parties may use cookies to deliver ads.
privacy.adsP2: Third-party vendors, including Google, may use cookies to serve ads based on your prior visits to this site or other websites.
privacy.adsP3: You can disable personalized ads in your Google account ad settings.
privacy.adsP4Suffix: to opt out of cookies used by third-party vendors for personalized advertising.
privacy.s5Body: We use Google Analytics. Google Analytics uses cookies to collect traffic data. Such data is collected in a form that does not personally identify you.
privacy.s6b1: We strive to keep site content accurate but do not warrant accuracy or safety.
privacy.s6b2: We are not liable for the accuracy of puzzle generation results or numeric data produced by the Service.
privacy.s6b3: We are not liable for any damage arising from use of content on this site.
privacy.s6b4: We are not responsible for information or services on external sites reached via links or banners.
privacy.s7Body: We comply with applicable Japanese laws regarding personal information and may revise this Policy from time to time. The latest version will always be posted on this page.
privacy.s8Intro: For questions about this Policy, please contact us at:

column.title: Educational column: digital logic puzzles and children’s thinking
column.introHeading: Introduction: interactive environments and Montessori auto-education
column.introP1: Wispo connects what digital media afford—immediate feedback, staged challenges, and low-cost trial-and-error—with Montessori ideas of auto-education and the prepared environment inside one play experience.
column.introP2: Software does not grade the child; it behaves as a transparent environment with consistent rules. Algorithmic generation echoes how adults sequence materials and tasks for developmental stages—without chasing buzzwords. It stands on durable ideas: trial, error, and correction; order and concentration; autonomy grounded in real mastery.
column.introP3: Below we survey Pair-Link, Reflec-Shot, Skyscraper, Pres-Sure Judge, and related content across design intent, cognitive neuroscience, Montessori practice, and difficulty curves—as talking points for parents and teachers.
column.tocPres: Pres-Sure Judge (プレッシャージャッジ)
column.s1pDev: Linking equal endpoints with a single path, covering all cells, and forbidding crossings is a long-standing pencil puzzle. Digitizing minimizes the cost of drawing, undoing, and retrying so thinking keeps its pace. Stylus and touch tie fine motor acts to spatial representations, linking to school-age graphic work.
column.s1pNeuro: Dual constraints (no crossings, full grid) repeatedly cue error prediction and strategy updating in the prefrontal cortex. Holding a provisional route while lookahead checks dead-ends loads spatial working memory, overlapping map-reading and unfolding-space reasoning. Thoughtful repetition encourages schema selection.
column.s1pMont: Brief demos show only the order (no crossings, no gaps) and leave route discovery to the child—bridging toward auto-education without hand-holding. Rule violations return instantly as interaction, offering correction independent of external judgment.
column.s1pStep: Grades (G1+) scale board size, pair count, and generator constraints. Early wins stabilize motivation; later grades widen the search space. Viewing lines as graph edges foreshadow discrete-math intuition.
column.s2pDev: Mirrors fascinate hands-on, yet lab setups are costly and noisy. Abstracting to a grid with wall and bumper symbols reproduces uniform motion and reflection digitally, safely cycling geometric intuition and symbolic reasoning.
column.s2pNeuro: Clear trajectories rehearse performance monitoring. Remembering turn sequences taxes working memory; committing a bumper after long-press + swipe couples motor planning with rapid decisions—near cognitive flexibility training.
column.s2pMont: Failure modes (facing a wall, returning to start, missing the goal) are explicit on-screen, echoing materials that make errors visible from the environment. Children align rules with their own moves, enabling self-evaluation with less adult interruption.
column.s2pStep: Grades grow board size and bumper complexity, moving from single-bounce prediction to multi-bounce inference. Early success supports normalized development of ego strength; later stages demand sustained attention and planning. The public lab suits small-scale observation before wider rollout.
column.s3pDev: Placing 1…N once per row and column while edge tallies match visible towers fuses Latin-square structure with visibility counting. Paper erasure is heavy; digital play lightens trials so effort stays on constraint reasoning.
column.s3pNeuro: Contradictory placements drive repeated prediction-error processing. Mental simulation of occlusion from above binds spatial inference to magnitude comparison. Maybe-mode externalizes working memory, scaffolding hypothesis versus commitment.
column.s3pMont: Order, precision, and concentration align with a quiet board that mirrors each action—external order nurturing inner order. Maybe-mode remains support; the child stays the agent.
column.s3pStep: Board size and presets widen the hypothesis space stepwise. Small boards make salient patterns tangible; large boards build stamina against compound constraints—parallel to sustained case analysis in school math.
column.s4Title: 4. Pres-Sure Judge (プレッシャージャッジ)
column.s4pDev: After the NPC places weights, players drag from inventory to pans under time pressure—hard to stage on paper with synchronized tilt and countdown. Digital coupling makes mistakes immediate; survival-style accumulation across rounds stresses strategy and self-regulation more than single-shot correctness.
column.s4pNeuro: Short windows for pan choice, weight choice, and Judge timing engage executive function—especially inhibitory control and rapid state updating—in the prefrontal cortex. Tracking the moving scale with the finger recruits visuomotor integration; holding numeric torque imagery doubles working-memory load. Frequent NPC moves drive prediction-error loops; longer sessions add arousal and sustained attention—training-like endurance for school-age executive skills.
column.s4pMont: Scale tilt offers concrete causality before abstract lecture—close to sensorial materials. Immediate feedback on misplacement or timeout supports correction from the environment, not scolding. The NPC models another agent’s move—a thin social frame for “how do I respond after someone else acts?” Reducing adult chatter while outcomes return through the environment aligns with auto-education.
column.s4pStep: Rounds shift NPC pressure and inventory tension, moving from single-weight comparison to multi-step balance imagery. The Judge action marks decision boundaries, separating impulse from inference. Gradual difficulty preserves a prepared gradient of load, integrating number, time, and movement beyond pure logic puzzles.
